name: agent-tool-builder
description: Tools are how AI agents interact with the world. A well-designed tool is the difference between an agent that works and one that hallucinates, fails silently, or costs 10x more tokens than necessary.  This skill covers tool design from schema to error handling. JSON Schema best practices, description writing that actually helps the LLM, validation, and the emerging MCP standard that's becoming the lingua franca for AI tools.  Key insight: Tool descriptions are more important than tool implementa
capabilities: Design clear JSON Schema definitions for agent tools
Write unambiguous tool descriptions that guide LLM behavior
Implement effective error handling for tool failures
Create input examples that demonstrate correct tool usage
Identify and avoid common tool design anti-patterns
author: sickn33
securitySummary: This skill is purely documentation providing guidance on agent tool design. Static analyzer flagged false positives: line 54 contains skill names (not shell commands), and cryptographic warnings matched keywords in prose (no actual code present). No executable code exists in this markdown file - it is instructional content only.
valueStatement: Poorly designed tool schemas cause agents to hallucinate, fail silently, or waste tokens. This skill teaches proven patterns for clear, unambiguous function-calling interfaces that work reliably.
limitations: Provides guidance and patterns, not executable tool implementations
Does not generate code automatically - requires manual implementation
Focuses on schema design rather than runtime tool orchestration
Best suited for developers building custom agent tools from scratch

useCases.targetUser: AI application developers building specialized agents
useCases.title: Build Custom Agent Tools
useCases.description: Design tool schemas for domain-specific operations like database queries, API integrations, or file operations that your agent needs to perform.

useCases.title: Improve Existing Function Calling
useCases.description: Refine poorly performing tools that cause hallucinations or inconsistent behavior by applying clear schema design principles.

useCases.targetUser: Platform teams building extensible agent frameworks
useCases.title: Implement MCP Tool Integration
useCases.description: Create Model Context Protocol compliant tools that enable agents to interact with external systems safely and predictably.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Tool Schema Review
promptTemplates.prompt: Review this tool schema and identify ambiguous descriptions or parameters that could cause inconsistent LLM behavior: [paste your schema]

promptTemplates.title: Error Response Design
promptTemplates.prompt: Design error response formats for a [tool name] that help the LLM understand what went wrong and how to recover or retry with corrected parameters.

promptTemplates.title: Tool Description Optimization
promptTemplates.prompt: Rewrite this tool description to be more specific about what the tool does, when to use it, and what outputs to expect: [paste current description]

promptTemplates.title: Complete Tool Set Design
promptTemplates.prompt: I need an agent to [describe goal]. Design a minimal set of 3-5 tools with clear schemas, descriptions, and example inputs that accomplish this without overlap or ambiguity.

bestPractices: Write descriptions that specify exactly when to use the tool and what it returns
Include concrete input examples showing typical parameter values
Design error responses that help the LLM understand and recover from failures
Limit tool sets to the minimum needed - too many options cause confusion
Use specific parameter names and types that match the domain vocabulary
antiPatterns: Vague descriptions like 'processes data' or 'handles requests' without specifics
Silent failures that return empty results instead of explicit error messages
Providing 20+ tools when 5-7 focused tools would be more effective
Schemas that accept any value without validation constraints
Descriptions that explain implementation instead of purpose and behavior

faq.question: How many tools should I provide to an agent?
faq.answer: Start with 5-7 focused tools. More than 10 often causes decision paralysis and increases token costs. Add tools only when existing ones cannot accomplish required tasks.

faq.question: What makes a tool description effective?
faq.answer: Effective descriptions state: what the tool does, when to use it, what parameters it expects, and what output format it returns. Avoid explaining implementation details.

faq.question: How do I handle tool errors gracefully?
faq.answer: Return structured errors with an error type, the specific field that failed, a human-readable message, and a suggestion for how to fix and retry the operation.

faq.question: Should I provide examples for every tool parameter?
faq.answer: Provide at least one complete example showing typical parameter values. For complex parameters or unusual formats, add specific examples for those fields.

faq.question: What is MCP and should I use it?
faq.answer: MCP (Model Context Protocol) is a standard for connecting AI models to tools and data sources. Use it when building tools that need to work across different AI platforms or with external systems.

faq.question: How do I test if my tool schemas work well?
faq.answer: Test with varied natural language prompts that should trigger the tool. Verify the LLM correctly identifies when to use it, provides valid parameters, and handles errors appropriately.
